1: 'Hear this word that the LORD has spoken against you, O people of Israel — against the whole family I brought up from the land of Egypt.' The summons to hear (shim'u et hadavar hazeh) is the standard prophetic call-to-attention formula. 'The whole family I brought up from the land of Egypt' — the specification that the indictment is against both halves of the divided kingdom ('the whole family', including Judah) reflects Amos's perspective that despite the political division, the covenant relationship is singular. The Exodus is again the ground for accountability.
2: 'You alone of all the families of the earth have I known (yadati) — therefore I will hold you accountable for all your iniquities.' The word yada (know) here means intimate covenant-knowledge, not mere factual awareness (cf. Genesis 4:1; Jeremiah 1:5). YHWH's exclusive covenant-knowledge of Israel is precisely what makes Israel's greater accountability inevitable — 'to whom much is given, much will be required' (Luke 12:48) is the underlying covenantal logic. This verse is Amos's theological foundation: special covenant status produces greater, not lesser, accountability. The Deuteronomic framework (Deuteronomy 7:6-11) assumed the same dynamic.
3: Amos now shifts to a series of wisdom-style rhetorical questions (vv3-8), each making the same point: every event has a cause; YHWH's prophetic word has a cause (his decision to act). 'Can two people walk together unless they have made an appointment (im lo noadau)?' — the image of two travelers who meet on the road only if they planned to meet; there are no coincidences in YHWH's dealings with Israel. The wisdom-question format connects Amos's prophetic activity to the wisdom tradition (cf. his self-identification as a 'dresser of sycamore figs', Amos 7:14, linking him to agricultural folk-wisdom).
4: 'Does a lion roar in the forest when it has no prey?' — the lion-roar of v2 (YHWH roaring from Zion) is now contextualized: a lion roars only when it is about to strike. The rhetorical question implies that YHWH's prophetic declaration (the lion's roar) is evidence that the strike is imminent. Lions were common in ancient Canaan (mentioned in 1 Kings 13:24; 2 Kings 17:25-26; Jeremiah 4:7; Amos 5:6); they were well-known as both agricultural threats and royal symbols (lion hunts were a standard royal activity in Assyrian bas-reliefs). The metaphor would have immediate resonance for a rural Tekoan shepherd like Amos.
5: The bird-and-snare metaphors continue the cause-and-effect series: a bird falling into a snare means a trap was set; a trap springing up means a bird triggered it. Both images make the same point: when disaster strikes, it has a cause. Applied to Amos's context: the disaster coming on Israel is not random but is YHWH's deliberate judgment triggered by Israel's specific sins. The trap-and-snare imagery for divine judgment appears also in Jeremiah (Amos 5:26-27) and Psalms (Amos 69:22; Amos 141:9), where the wicked's traps are turned against them.
6: 'When a ram's horn (shofar) sounds the alarm in a city, do the people not tremble? When disaster strikes a city, has not the LORD brought it about?' The shofar as a warning instrument (Numbers 10:5-9; Nehemiah 4:20) and the attribution of urban catastrophe to YHWH's agency both connect to the covenant-curse framework of Deuteronomy 28 and Leviticus 26. The affirmation 'has not the LORD brought it about (im YHWH lo asah)?' is the theological claim that drives Amos's entire prophetic activity: historical disasters are YHWH's judgments, not random misfortune or the work of other gods.
7: 'For the Lord GOD does nothing without first disclosing his plans (sodo) to his servants the prophets.' The word sod (council, counsel, secret counsel) refers to the divine council — the heavenly assembly where YHWH makes his decisions — into which the prophet is admitted as an observer (cf. 1 Kings 22:19-23; Jeremiah 23:18-22; Isaiah 6:1-13). The prophet is YHWH's ambassador who reports to humanity what was decided in the divine council; prophecy is not private inspiration but participation in the heavenly deliberations. This verse grounds prophetic authority in access to YHWH's own counsel.
8: 'The lion has roared — who will not be afraid? The Lord GOD has spoken — who can withhold prophecy?' The paralleled structure equates YHWH's speech with the lion's roar (as in v4). The compulsion of prophecy — the inability to withhold it when YHWH has spoken — is the inner experience of the prophetic vocation (cf. Jeremiah 20:9: 'there is something like a burning fire shut up in my bones... I am weary of holding it in, and I cannot'). Amos's defense of his prophetic activity (Amos 7:14-15: 'the LORD took me from following the flock and said to me, Go, prophesy') reflects exactly this compulsion.
9: 'Proclaim to the citadels of Ashdod and to the strongholds in the land of Egypt: Gather on the mountains of Samaria.' The irony of summoning the pagan nations (Philistia and Egypt) to witness Israel's moral disorder is biting: the nations renowned for their own injustice (Ashdod was condemned in Amos 1) are invited to observe that Israel's internal social conditions are worse. The 'mountains of Samaria' (Har Shomron) were the elevated terrain around the capital, where the wealthy built their second homes (cf. Amos 4:1: cows of Bashan on Mount Samaria). The foreign nations are cast as witnesses at Israel's trial.
10: 'They do not know how to act with integrity (lo yad'u asot nachona), declares the LORD — those who hoard violence and plunder in their fortresses.' The inability to 'do right' is not merely moral failure but a kind of moral blindness — they do not know, cannot perceive, what right action looks like. The accumulation of 'violence and plunder' (chamas veshed) in the fortress-citadels is the physical evidence of structural injustice: the wealth stored in the elite residences came from the exploitation documented in Amos 2:6-8.
11: 'Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: An enemy will surround the land; he will strip away your defense and plunder your fortresses.' The 'enemy' (tzar) is left unnamed — Amos does not yet name Assyria explicitly in this oracle, though it is the obvious referent given the political situation. The stripping away of defenses and plundering of fortresses directly reverses the accumulation condemned in v10: the fortresses filled with plunder will themselves be plundered. This reversal-of-plunder justice is characteristic of the covenant-curse framework (Leviticus 26:33; Deuteronomy 28:33).
12: 'As the shepherd snatches from the lion's mouth two legs or a scrap of an ear, so will the Israelites living in Samaria be rescued — with the corner of a bed and a scrap of couch.' The shepherd saving two legs or an ear from a lion-kill was a legal procedure for proving that the animal had died rather than been stolen (Exodus 22:13); the fragments served as evidence. Amos's ironic use of this image: the 'rescue' of Israel will be as pitiful as the fragments that prove something was consumed — not a real salvation but the evidence that something died. The 'corner of a bed and a scrap of couch' specifically mocks the ivory-inlaid luxury furniture condemned in Amos 6:4.
13: 'Hear this and testify against the house of Jacob, declares the Lord GOD, the God of armies.' The divine title Elohim Tzvaot (God of armies/hosts) appears in Amos four times; it emphasizes the military sovereignty of YHWH over all human armies — the same 'armies' that Israel's wealthy class trusted in their fortresses are under YHWH's command. The summons to testify (ve'ha'idu) continues the juridical language that runs through Amos 3: Israel is on trial, and the nations (v9) and now an unnamed witness are called to testify against Jacob.
14: 'On the day I call Israel to account (paqdi al pish'ei Yisrael), I will also strike down the altars of Bethel; the horns of the altar will be cut off and fall to the ground.' The altar-horns at Bethel are the specific cultic infrastructure at the northern kingdom's primary sanctuary. The horns of the altar were the projection of divine protection — grasping the altar-horns was the classic act of seeking asylum (1 Kings 1:50; Amos 2:28). YHWH striking the horns off eliminates both the cult and the asylum-function simultaneously: there is no refuge in the altar of a corrupt cult.
15: 'I will demolish both the winter house and the summer house; the houses adorned with ivory will perish and the great houses will be swept away.' The archaeological record from 8th-century Samaria confirms both the luxury described here and its eventual destruction. The Samaria Ivories — over 500 ivory fragments found at the excavation of ancient Samaria — were plaques and decorative panels from elite furniture exactly like that described in Amos 3:15 and Amos 6:4 (beds and couches inlaid with ivory). The 'winter house and summer house' are the seasonal residences of the wealthy, confirmed in both Iron Age archaeology and Egyptian and Assyrian administrative texts.
